sections.heading: The Nestorian Controversy
sections.html:
The Nestorian Controversy

The Council of Ephesus (AD 431), the third ecumenical council, was called to resolve a controversy ignited by Nestorius, Archbishop of Constantinople. Nestorius objected to the title Theotokos (Θεοτόκος — "God-bearer" or "Mother of God") given to Mary in popular devotion, preferring instead Christotokos ("Christ-bearer").

The dispute was not primarily about Mary but about Christology — specifically, how the divine and human natures of Christ are united. Nestorius appeared to teach that the divine Word and the human Jesus were two distinct subjects (persons) joined in a moral or relational union, not one unified Person. In this view, Mary bore the human Jesus but not the divine Son — hence the dispute over her title.

Cyril of Alexandria led the opposition, insisting on the unity of Christ's person: the same subject who was born of Mary is the eternal Son of God. Therefore calling Mary Theotokos is not worship of Mary but a confession of Christ's Incarnation. Emperor Theodosius II called a council at Ephesus to settle the matter.

The council was bitterly contested. Cyril arrived with his Egyptian bishops and, without waiting for the Syrian bishops under John of Antioch who supported Nestorius, opened the proceedings and condemned Nestorius. When the Syrian bishops arrived days later they held a rival counter-council condemning Cyril. Eventually the imperial government sided with Cyril, and Nestorius was deposed and exiled.

sections.heading: The Definition: Mary as Theotokos
sections.html:
The Definition: Mary as Theotokos

The council's central act was to confirm Cyril's second letter to Nestorius as the standard of orthodoxy, and to anathematize Nestorius's contrary teaching. The key theological definition from Cyril's letter affirmed at the council:

We do not say that the nature of the Word was changed and became flesh, or that it was converted into a whole man consisting of soul and body; but rather that the Word, having personally united to himself flesh animated by a rational soul, did in an ineffable and incomprehensible manner become man, and was called the Son of Man, not merely as willing or being pleased to be so called, not simply as putting on a human form, but because the two natures being brought together in a true union, there is of both one Christ and one Son; for the difference of the natures is not taken away by the union, but rather the divinity and the humanity make perfect the one Lord Jesus Christ by their ineffable and mysterious concurrence into unity.

The title Theotokos was confirmed not as a statement about Mary but as the necessary implication of this Christology:

If anyone does not confess that the Emmanuel is very God, and that therefore the Holy Virgin is Theotokos (for she bare after the flesh the Word of God become flesh), let him be anathema.

The theological principle is: because Christ is one Person (not two), whatever is true of his human birth is predicable of the Word himself. The Word of God was born, not merely the human Jesus. (Luke 1:43; John 1:14; Rom. 9:5; Gal. 4:4)

sections.heading: The Twelve Anathemas of Cyril
sections.html:
The Twelve Anathemas of Cyril

Attached to Cyril's third letter to Nestorius were twelve anathemas that summarize the Ephesine Christology. Several of the most significant:

1. If anyone will not confess that the Emmanuel is very God, and that on this account the Holy Virgin is the Mother of God (Theotokos), inasmuch as in the flesh she bore the Word of God made flesh — let him be anathema.

2. If anyone shall not confess that the Word of God the Father is united hypostatically to flesh, and that with that flesh of his own, he is one Christ — both God and man at the same time — let him be anathema.

3. If anyone shall divide between the two persons or subsistences those expressions which are contained in the evangelical and apostolical writings, or which have been said concerning Christ by the saints, or by himself, and shall apply some to him as to a man separate from the Word of God, and shall apply others to the only Word of God the Father, on the ground that they are fit to be applied to God alone — let him be anathema.

4. If anyone shall say that Christ was a God-bearing man, and not rather that he is very God, as an only Son through nature, inasmuch as the Word was made flesh, and shared in like manner our blood and flesh — let him be anathema.

11. If anyone shall not confess that the flesh of the Lord is life-giving and that it pertains to the Word of God the Father himself, but shall pretend that it belongs to another person who is united with him merely in honour... and has not rather confessed that that flesh is life-giving because it became the very own of the Word who is able to quicken all things — let him be anathema. (John 6:51; John 6:55–57)

12. If anyone shall not acknowledge that the Word of God suffered in the flesh, that he was crucified in the flesh, and that likewise in that same flesh he tasted death... let him be anathema. (1 Pet. 4:1; Heb. 2:9)
